sets.passage: Listen to a conversation between a student and a librarian. Student: Hi, I think I left a silver USB drive at one of the computer desks yesterday afternoon. It has all my thesis notes on it. Librarian: Let me check the lost-and-found box behind the desk. We log everything found after closing. Do you remember which floor? Student: The third floor, near the printers. Librarian: Okay, I see two drives here. One is black, and one is silver with a small blue sticker. Student: Yes! The blue sticker is mine. Thank you so much. I was panicking all morning. Librarian: I understand completely. In the future, you might email a backup to yourself, just in case. Student: That's a good idea. Actually, could you show me how the library backs up files on the shared computers? Librarian: The shared machines wipe everything overnight, so they don't keep copies. Always save to your own drive or the cloud.

questions.prompt: Why does the student go to the librarian?
options: To recover a lost USB drive

questions.prompt: How does the librarian identify the correct drive?
options: By a blue sticker on it

questions.prompt: What can be inferred about the shared library computers?
options: They are only on the third floor
They do not retain files overnight

sets.explain_zh: 学生来图书馆找回丢失的U盘，馆员靠蓝色贴纸辨认，并说明公用电脑每晚清空文件不保存。

sets.passage: Listen to a conversation between a student and an academic advisor. Student: I signed up for Statistics and Organic Chemistry, but I just realized both lectures meet Tuesday at ten. Advisor: That's a direct conflict, so you can't keep both. Which one matters more for your plan? Student: Chemistry is required for my major, but Statistics is only offered this semester. Advisor: Actually, Statistics runs again in spring with an evening section. Chemistry, though, has just this one slot until next year. Student: Then I should keep Chemistry now and take Statistics in spring. Advisor: I'd agree. Waiting on Chemistry would push your whole major sequence back a year. Student: That would be a disaster. Can you help me drop Statistics today? Advisor: Of course. I'll process the drop, and I'll add a note reminding you to register early for the spring evening section, since it fills quickly. Student: Perfect. Thanks for sorting this out so fast.

questions.prompt: What problem does the student bring to the advisor?
options: Two courses meet at the same time

questions.prompt: Why does the advisor recommend keeping Chemistry?
options: It is offered only once until next year

questions.prompt: What does the advisor suggest about the spring Statistics section?
options: The student should register early because it fills quickly

sets.explain_zh: 学生两门课时间冲突，顾问建议先修一年只开一次的化学，春季再修统计，并提醒尽早选课。

sets.passage: Listen to a conversation between a student and a billing office clerk. Student: I'm looking at my account and it shows the lab fee charged twice this month. Clerk: Let me pull up your statement. You're right, there are two identical charges of forty dollars on the same date. Student: I only registered for one lab, so I don't understand it. Clerk: It looks like a system glitch during the registration update. It duplicated several students' fees that afternoon. Student: So will I get the extra forty dollars back? Clerk: Yes. I'll submit a correction now, and the credit should appear within three business days. Student: Will it come back as a refund or a credit toward next term? Clerk: Since your balance is already paid, it'll be refunded to the card you used. Student: Great. Should I check back if it doesn't show up? Clerk: If you don't see it by Friday, call this office and reference the ticket number I'm giving you now.

questions.prompt: What is the student's complaint?
options: A wrong course on the bill

questions.prompt: What does the clerk suggest caused the duplicate charge?
options: A system glitch during a registration update

questions.prompt: How will the extra money be returned?
options: As a credit for next term
As a refund to the student's card

sets.explain_zh: 学生发现实验费被重复扣款，职员解释是系统故障导致，将退回到原支付卡上。

sets.passage: Listen to a conversation between a student and a housing office staff member. Student: I'd like to request a room change. My current roommate practices trumpet until midnight, and I can't sleep. Staff: I'm sorry to hear that. Have you tried talking to your roommate first? Student: Twice. He says he has no other place to practice. Staff: Fair enough. We do have a few single rooms opening next week in the north building. Student: Is there an extra charge for a single? Staff: Yes, singles cost about two hundred dollars more per term. But there's also a double in the quiet-study dorm, same price as now. Student: The quiet dorm sounds perfect, and I'd rather not pay more. Staff: Good choice. Quiet-dorm residents agree to keep noise low after nine, so the trumpet issue won't repeat. I'll start the transfer paperwork, and you can move this weekend. Student: Thank you. That's a huge relief.

questions.prompt: Why does the student want to change rooms?
Why does the student choose the quiet-study dorm instead of a single?
options: It costs the same as the current room

questions.prompt: What can be inferred about the quiet-study dorm?
options: It has no roommates at all
It is more expensive than a single
It is only for graduate students

sets.explain_zh: 学生因室友深夜练小号申请换宿，选择与现价相同的安静宿舍，那里有更严格的噪音规定。

sets.passage: Listen to a conversation between a student and a registrar's office clerk. Student: I got a C on my history final grade, but my professor told me I earned a B-plus. Clerk: Grade disputes go through a specific process. Have you contacted the professor directly? Student: Yes, and she agrees it's a mistake. She said she'd submit a change. Clerk: If the professor has already emailed the correction, I can look for it in the pending queue. Let me search. Student: Was anything filed? Clerk: I see a grade-change request from Professor Lin, submitted two days ago, still awaiting the department chair's signature. Student: How long does the chair's approval usually take? Clerk: Normally three to five business days. Once it's signed, the new grade posts automatically to your transcript. Student: Will the old C affect my scholarship review next week? Clerk: I'll flag your file as pending correction, so the review committee won't act on the incorrect grade. Student: That's a relief. Thank you.

questions.prompt: What is the student trying to resolve?
What is the current status of the grade change?
options: It is awaiting the department chair's signature

questions.prompt: Why does the clerk flag the student's file?
options: So the review committee won't act on the wrong grade

sets.explain_zh: 学生的历史成绩记错，教授已提交改分申请等系主任签字，职员标记档案以免奖学金评审误用错误成绩。

sets.passage: Listen to a conversation between a student and a photography club officer. Student: Hi, I saw your flyer. How do I join the photography club? Officer: Great to hear you're interested. Membership is free, but we ask everyone to attend one intro workshop first. Student: Do I need my own camera? Officer: Not at all. We have six loaner cameras, and the workshop teaches you how to use them. Student: When's the next workshop? Officer: This Thursday at six, in the arts building, room two-ten. Student: Perfect. Is there a limit on how many photos I can enter in the annual show? Officer: Members can submit up to three prints, and the club covers the printing cost for the first one. Student: That's generous. What if I've never developed film before? Officer: Then you'll love our darkroom sessions on Fridays. Beginners always start there. Just sign this sheet, and I'll email you the workshop details tonight.

questions.prompt: What must the student do before becoming an active member?
What does the club provide for members without a camera?
What can be inferred about the club's attitude toward beginners?

sets.explain_zh: 学生咨询加入摄影社，需先参加入门工作坊，社团提供借用相机并欢迎新手，态度友好。

sets.passage: Listen to a conversation between a student and a lab technician. Student: The spectrometer in room four keeps giving error code twelve whenever I start a scan. Technician: Error twelve usually means the lamp needs warming up. Did you wait the full fifteen minutes after switching it on? Student: I waited maybe five. I was in a hurry. Technician: That's likely the whole problem. The lamp must stabilize, or the readings drift and it throws that error. Student: Oh. So there's nothing broken? Technician: Probably not, but let me check the log. Hmm, the machine also reports the calibration is overdue by a week. Student: Does that matter for my samples? Technician: It could shift your absorbance values slightly. I'll recalibrate it now, and you can rerun your scan afterward. Student: How long will calibration take? Technician: About twenty minutes. Come back at eleven, give the lamp its full warm-up, and you should get clean data. Student: Thanks, I'll be more patient this time.

questions.prompt: What error is the student experiencing?
What does the technician imply the student did wrong?
options: Did not wait for the lamp to warm up fully

questions.prompt: What additional issue does the technician discover?

sets.explain_zh: 学生用分光光度计报错，技工指出灯未充分预热是主因，并发现校准逾期，将重新校准。

sets.passage: Listen to a conversation between a student and a bookstore clerk. Student: I need to return this economics textbook. I dropped the course this morning. Clerk: No problem, as long as it's within the return window. Do you have your receipt? Student: Here it is. I bought it four days ago. Clerk: You're within the seven-day period, so you're fine. Is the book still in new condition? Student: I only opened it once, and I didn't write in it. Clerk: Let me inspect it. The cover and pages look untouched, so we can give a full refund. Student: Wonderful. Also, I might need the sociology text instead. Do you have it? Clerk: We're out of new copies, but there's a used one for half price behind me. Student: I'll take the used one. Can I apply my refund toward it? Clerk: Absolutely. I'll deduct the price of the used book from your refund and return the difference to your card.

questions.prompt: Why is the student returning the textbook?
What condition allows a full refund?
options: The book being in new, unwritten condition

questions.prompt: What does the student decide to do with the refund?
options: Apply part of it toward a used sociology book

sets.explain_zh: 学生退经济学课本因退课，书为全新可全额退款，并用退款抵扣一本半价二手社会学书。

sets.passage: Listen to a conversation between a student and a recreation center employee. Student: My work schedule changed, so I can't make the Monday swim class anymore. Can I switch? Employee: Sure. What times are you now free? Student: Wednesday or Friday mornings work best. Employee: We have an intermediate class Wednesday at seven and a beginner one Friday at eight. Student: I've swum for years, so intermediate is right. Employee: Then Wednesday at seven it is. There's no fee to transfer within the same term. Student: Good. Will my remaining sessions carry over? Employee: Yes, you had six left in the Monday class, and all six move to Wednesday. Student: What about the instructor? I liked coach Rivera. Employee: Coach Rivera actually teaches the Wednesday intermediate class too, so you'll have the same coach. Student: That's perfect. Please move me over. Employee: Done. You'll get a confirmation email, and your first Wednesday session is this week.

questions.prompt: Why does the student want to switch swim classes?
options: The pool is closed on Mondays

questions.prompt: Which class does the student choose?
What can be inferred that pleases the student most about the switch?

sets.explain_zh: 学生因工作时间变动申请换游泳课，选周三中级班，剩余课次可结转，且教练相同令其满意。

sets.passage: Listen to a conversation between a student and a career center counselor. Student: I'd like to sign up for the resume workshop before the job fair next month. Counselor: Good timing. We run two sessions, one for first drafts and one for polishing. Which fits you? Student: I already have a draft, but it feels weak. Counselor: Then the polishing session is better for you. It focuses on wording and layout. Student: When does it meet? Counselor: Next Tuesday at four. Bring a printed copy and a specific job posting you're targeting. Student: Why the job posting? Counselor: We tailor your resume to match the exact skills each employer lists. Generic resumes get overlooked. Student: That makes sense. Is there space left? Counselor: Two seats remain, so I'll reserve one for you now. Also, book a one-on-one review afterward if you want deeper feedback. Student: I'll do both. Thank you for the advice.

questions.prompt: Which workshop session does the counselor recommend?
Why should the student bring a specific job posting?
options: To tailor the resume to the employer's listed skills

questions.prompt: What is the counselor's attitude toward generic resumes?

sets.explain_zh: 学生报名简历打磨工作坊，需带招聘信息以便按雇主要求定制，顾问认为泛用简历易被忽视。

sets.passage: Listen to a conversation between a student and an IT help desk technician. Student: I can't log into the campus network on my laptop, but my phone connects fine. Technician: That's a useful clue. When did the laptop stop working? Student: After I changed my password yesterday. Technician: There it is. Your laptop probably saved the old password and keeps trying it. Student: How do I fix that? Technician: Open your network settings, forget the campus network, then reconnect with the new password. Student: Let me try. Okay, it's asking for the password now. Technician: Type the new one carefully. Passwords here are case-sensitive. Student: It connected! That was simple. Technician: Devices cache old credentials all the time. Whenever you change a password, update every device you own. Student: Good to know. Should I update the library computers too? Technician: No, those log you in fresh each time, so they'll use the new password automatically. Student: Thanks for the quick fix.

questions.prompt: What is the student's problem?
options: The laptop won't log into the network

questions.prompt: What does the technician conclude caused the laptop's issue?
options: It kept using the old saved password

questions.prompt: What general advice does the technician give?
options: Update every device after a password change

sets.explain_zh: 学生笔记本无法联网，因改密码后仍用旧密码，技工建议忘记网络重连，并提醒改密后更新所有设备。

sets.passage: Listen to a conversation between a student and a dining services coordinator. Student: I want to change my meal plan. The nineteen-meal plan is way too much food. Coordinator: A lot of students find that. What's your typical eating pattern? Student: I skip breakfast most days and eat out on weekends. Coordinator: Then the ten-meal plan with dining dollars might suit you better. Student: How do dining dollars work? Coordinator: They're a flexible balance you spend at any campus cafe, not tied to full meals. Student: So I could grab just a coffee and a muffin? Coordinator: Exactly. It's better for lighter or irregular eaters like you. Student: Is there a deadline to switch? Coordinator: You have until Friday. After that, plans lock for the semester. Student: Will I lose the meals I've already used this week? Coordinator: No, we prorate the change, so you only pay for what's ahead. Student: Then please switch me to the ten-meal plan today.

questions.prompt: Why does the student want to change the meal plan?
options: The nineteen-meal plan is too much food

questions.prompt: What advantage of dining dollars does the coordinator mention?
options: They can be spent flexibly on any item

questions.prompt: What happens to meals already used before switching?
options: The change is prorated so only future meals are paid for

sets.explain_zh: 学生觉得餐次太多想改餐计划，协调员推荐含灵活餐费的十餐计划，改动按比例结算，周五前须完成。

sets.passage: Listen to a conversation between a student and a professor during office hours. Student: Professor, I wanted to ask for a short extension on the research essay. Professor: What's going on? The deadline is Friday. Student: My grandmother was hospitalized, and I've been traveling to see her all week. Professor: I'm sorry to hear that. Family comes first. How much more time do you need? Student: Would Monday be possible? Professor: Monday works. I'll note the extension so the late penalty doesn't apply. Student: Thank you. Should I email you the essay or upload it? Professor: Upload it to the course portal, same as everyone. I'll just adjust your due date there. Student: Understood. One more thing, I'm worried my sources are too old. Professor: Let me see your list. These are fine, but add two studies from the last five years to show current research. Student: I'll do that over the weekend. Thanks for being so understanding.

questions.prompt: What does the student request from the professor?
options: A short extension on an essay

questions.prompt: Why does the professor grant the request without penalty?
options: A family emergency kept the student away

questions.prompt: What does the professor advise about the student's sources?
options: Add two recent studies from the last five years

sets.explain_zh: 学生因祖母住院请求延期，教授准许且免罚，并建议增加近五年的两项研究以体现最新成果。

sets.passage: Listen to a conversation between a student and a financial aid officer. Student: My scholarship hasn't shown up in my account, and rent is due soon. Officer: Let me look. Your scholarship was approved, but the funds are held pending one missing document. Student: Which document? Officer: We need your signed enrollment verification form. Without it, the system won't release the money. Student: I don't remember getting that form. Officer: It was emailed in June. It probably went to your spam folder. I can print you a copy right now. Student: Please do. If I sign it today, when will the money arrive? Officer: Once you submit it, disbursement takes about two business days. Student: That's cutting it close for rent. Officer: If timing is tight, you can request a short-term emergency loan from this office to bridge the gap. Student: I might need that. Let me sign the form first and see. Officer: Sensible. Here's the form and a pen.

questions.prompt: Why hasn't the student's scholarship money arrived?
options: A signed enrollment verification form is missing

questions.prompt: Where was the missing form likely sent?
What backup option does the officer offer?

sets.explain_zh: 学生奖学金因缺少签字的注册确认表被扣发，该表误入垃圾邮件，职员补印并提议紧急短期贷款应急。

sets.passage: Listen to a conversation between a student and a parking services clerk. Student: I'd like to buy a parking permit for the fall semester. Clerk: Do you live on campus or commute? Student: I commute from about twenty minutes away. Clerk: Then you'll want a commuter permit, which lets you park in lots C and D. Student: Are those close to the science building? Clerk: Lot D is a two-minute walk. Lot C is farther but rarely full. Student: How much is the permit? Clerk: A hundred and twenty dollars for the term, or you can pay monthly at twenty-five. Student: The term rate is cheaper overall, so I'll do that. Clerk: Good choice. One rule: permits don't guarantee a space, so arrive early on busy mornings. Student: What counts as busy? Clerk: Before ten most weekdays. After that, Lot D fills up. I'll register your license plate now and email the digital permit.

questions.prompt: What type of permit does the student need?
Why does the student choose the term rate?
options: It is cheaper overall than monthly

questions.prompt: What does the clerk warn the student about?
options: Arriving early is wise since spaces aren't guaranteed

sets.explain_zh: 学生买通勤停车证，选更划算的整学期费率，职员提醒证不保证车位，繁忙上午需早到。

sets.passage: Listen to a conversation between a student and a writing center tutor. Student: I'd like to book an appointment to go over my scholarship application essay. Tutor: Sure. Is this a first look or a revision? Student: It's a first draft, and I'm not sure my main point is clear. Tutor: Clarity of the main point is exactly what a first session targets. How long is the essay? Student: About seven hundred words. Tutor: That fits a thirty-minute slot. We have openings Wednesday afternoon and Thursday morning. Student: Thursday morning is better. Should I bring anything? Tutor: Bring the prompt and your draft, printed if possible. We read on paper here. Student: Will you fix my grammar too? Tutor: We focus on ideas and structure first. Grammar comes in a later session, once your argument is solid. Student: That order makes sense. Book me for Thursday at nine, please. Tutor: Done. You'll get a reminder the night before.

questions.prompt: What kind of session does the student book?
options: A first-draft session on the essay's main point

questions.prompt: What does the tutor ask the student to bring?
options: The prompt and a printed draft

questions.prompt: Why does the tutor postpone grammar to a later session?
options: Ideas and structure are addressed before grammar

sets.explain_zh: 学生预约写作中心看奖学金申请文初稿，重点在主旨清晰，需带打印稿，语法留待后续。

sets.passage: Listen to a conversation between a student and a study abroad advisor. Student: I want to apply for the spring program in Lisbon, but I'm confused about the language requirement. Advisor: The Lisbon program is taught in English, so you don't need Portuguese to enroll. Student: Oh, good. I was worried. Do I still need any language course? Advisor: You're encouraged to take a beginner survival-Portuguese class, but it's optional. Student: What about the application deadline? Advisor: It's October fifteenth, and late applications aren't accepted, since housing is arranged in advance. Student: What documents do I need? Advisor: Two things: a one-page statement of purpose and a copy of your passport. Student: My passport expires in March. Is that a problem? Advisor: Yes, it must be valid for six months beyond the program's end. You'll need to renew it first. Student: I'll start the renewal this week, then. Advisor: Do that early. Passport renewals can take weeks.

questions.prompt: What is the student confused about at first?
Why must the student renew the passport?
options: It must be valid six months beyond the program's end

questions.prompt: What does the advisor imply about the application deadline?
options: It only applies to some students
It is firm because housing is arranged ahead

sets.explain_zh: 学生咨询里斯本项目，课程为英语无需葡语，但护照须在项目结束后仍有效六个月，需尽早续签。

sets.passage: Listen to a conversation between a student and a health center receptionist. Student: I'd like to make an appointment for a persistent cough that's lasted two weeks. Receptionist: Two weeks is worth checking. Are you having trouble breathing or a fever? Student: No fever, and breathing is fine. It's just annoying and won't go away. Receptionist: Okay, that's a routine visit rather than urgent care. Our earliest opening is tomorrow at two. Student: Tomorrow works. Do I need to bring anything? Receptionist: Just your student ID and a list of any medications you take. Student: I take an allergy pill each morning. Receptionist: Note that for the doctor, since allergies can cause a lingering cough. Student: I hadn't thought of that. Is there a visit fee? Receptionist: Routine visits are covered by your student health fee, so there's no charge. Student: Great. Book me for two tomorrow, please. Receptionist: Done. Arrive ten minutes early to fill out a short form.

questions.prompt: Why does the student visit the health center?
Why is the visit classified as routine rather than urgent?
options: The student has a high fever
There is no fever or breathing trouble

questions.prompt: What does the receptionist suggest the cough might be linked to?

sets.explain_zh: 学生因两周久咳预约就诊，无发烧属常规就诊，前台提醒久咳可能与其过敏有关，费用已由健康费覆盖。

sets.passage: Listen to a conversation between a student and a music department staff member. Student: I need to reserve a practice room for my recital preparation. Staff: How often do you plan to practice? Student: About two hours every evening for the next month. Staff: For a recital, you can book a priority room with a grand piano. Regular rooms only have uprights. Student: A grand would be ideal, since I'm performing on one. Staff: Priority rooms require your instructor's signature confirming the recital date. Student: I can bring that tomorrow. Is there a booking limit? Staff: Priority holders may reserve up to two hours a day, which matches your plan perfectly. Student: Great. Can I always use the same room? Staff: Yes, we assign recital students a fixed room so you get used to its acoustics. Student: That's thoughtful. Which room would I get? Staff: Room fifteen, on the quiet end of the hall. Bring the signed form and I'll finalize it.

questions.prompt: Why does the student want a priority practice room?
options: To prepare for a recital on a grand piano

questions.prompt: What is required to book a priority room?
options: The instructor's signature confirming the recital

questions.prompt: Why are recital students given a fixed room?
options: So they get used to the room's acoustics

sets.explain_zh: 学生为音乐会预约带三角钢琴的优先练习室，需老师签字确认，且固定房间以熟悉音响效果。

sets.passage: Listen to a conversation between a student and a registrar's office clerk. Student: I need an official transcript sent to a company for a summer internship. Clerk: Sure. Official transcripts can go electronically or by mail. Which does the company want? Student: The application says an electronic copy sent directly from the school. Clerk: Then I'll use our secure e-delivery. What's the recipient's email? Student: It's on this form here. Clerk: Got it. There's a small fee, five dollars per electronic transcript. Student: That's fine. How soon will it arrive? Clerk: Electronic transcripts are usually delivered within an hour. Student: Perfect, because the deadline is tonight. Clerk: You're well within it, then. One note: your current-semester grades post next week, so this transcript won't include them yet. Student: The internship only needs my completed courses, so that's okay. Clerk: Then you're all set. I'll send it now and email you a confirmation receipt. Student: Thank you so much.

questions.prompt: Why does the student need a transcript?
options: To apply for a summer internship

questions.prompt: How does the company want the transcript delivered?
What does the clerk note about the transcript's contents?
options: It won't yet include current-semester grades

sets.explain_zh: 学生为暑期实习需学校直接电子发送的官方成绩单，一小时内送达，但不含尚未录入的本学期成绩。

sets.passage: Listen to a conversation between two students. Student A: Hey, did you go to yesterday's biology lecture? I was home sick. Student B: I did. It was all about cell respiration, and the professor moved fast. Student A: Could I borrow your notes to catch up? Student B: Sure, but honestly my notes are messy. Why don't we form a quick study group instead? Student A: That sounds better. Who else? Student B: Maya and Chen are both behind too. We could meet at the library tonight. Student A: Tonight works. Should we each cover a topic? Student B: Good idea. You take respiration since you missed it, so you'll learn it deeply by teaching us. Student A: Teaching it back does force you to understand it. Fair. Student B: Right. I'll book a group room for seven. Bring the textbook, chapter nine. Student A: Perfect. Thanks for turning my absence into something useful. Student B: That's what classmates are for.

questions.prompt: Why did Student A miss the biology lecture?
What does Student B suggest instead of just lending notes?
Why does Student B assign the respiration topic to Student A?
options: Teaching it will help Student A learn it deeply
It was not covered in class

sets.explain_zh: 两名同学因A缺课，B提议组学习小组而非只借笔记，并让A讲呼吸作用以通过教学更深理解。

sets.passage: Listen to a conversation between a student and a professor. Student: Professor, I'm enrolled in the Thursday lab, but I just got a part-time job with Thursday shifts. Professor: I see. So you'd like to move to a different lab section. Student: Yes, is the Tuesday section open? Professor: Let me check enrollment. Tuesday has two open seats, so a switch is possible. Student: That's a relief. Would I miss anything by moving? Professor: The Tuesday section is actually one experiment ahead. You'd need to make up the enzyme lab you'd skip. Student: How do I make it up? Professor: Come to my Thursday section this week just for that experiment, then start Tuesday next week. Student: So I do the enzyme lab once, then switch permanently. Professor: Exactly. I'll email the Tuesday instructor so she expects you. Student: Thank you. This solves my scheduling clash. Professor: Happy to help. Just don't fall behind on the lab reports during the transition.

questions.prompt: Why does the student want to switch lab sections?
options: A part-time job conflicts with Thursdays

questions.prompt: What must the student do because the Tuesday section is ahead?
What does the professor caution the student about?
options: Not falling behind on lab reports during the transition

sets.explain_zh: 学生因兼职周四班冲突想转周二实验班，因进度超前需补做酶实验，教授提醒过渡期别落下实验报告。

sets.passage: Listen to a conversation between a student and a librarian. Student: I need an article that our library doesn't have. Can you get it somehow? Librarian: Yes, through interlibrary loan. Do you have the full citation? Student: I have the title, authors, and journal name, but not the exact page numbers. Librarian: That's usually enough for us to locate it. Is it a scanned article or a physical book? Student: It's a journal article. Librarian: Then it'll come as a PDF, emailed to you, often within three to five days. Student: My paper is due in a week, so that should work. Librarian: It should. There's no fee for article scans, only for physical book loans from far away. Student: Good. Is there a limit on requests? Librarian: You can request up to twenty items a semester, and you've used none. Student: Then please submit this one. Librarian: Filling it out now. Watch your email for the download link.

questions.prompt: What service does the student use?

sets.passage: Listen to a conversation between a student and an academic advisor. Student: I'm thinking about adding a minor in environmental science. Advisor: That pairs well with your economics major. What's driving your interest? Student: I want to work in sustainability policy after graduation. Advisor: Then the minor is a smart fit. It's six courses, and two overlap with your major requirements. Student: So I'd only add four new courses? Advisor: Correct. That's very manageable over your remaining two years. Student: Would it delay my graduation? Advisor: Not if you start next semester. Wait longer, and the required intro course fills up fast. Student: I'll register for it in the first round, then. Advisor: Wise. One more thing: the minor requires a field project in your senior year. Student: Is that a big commitment? Advisor: About one afternoon a week for a semester, but employers love it. Student: That settles it. Please add the minor to my plan. Advisor: Done, and I'll flag the intro course for you.

It can be done in one term

How can I tell which dishes are safe? Manager: That's a common worry, and we take it seriously. Every station now has a printed card listing the main allergens in each dish, and anything containing nuts is marked with a small red symbol. And download our dining app — you can filter the menu to hide anything with nuts before you even arrive.

Clerk: Let me pull up your record. Which course did you drop? Student: Introduction to Psychology, on the tenth. Clerk: I see the drop, and it was within the full-refund window, so you're owed the tuition back. Student: Then why hasn't it arrived? Clerk: It looks like the refund was issued as an account credit, not returned to your card. Student: But I paid with a loan, not out of pocket. Clerk: Ah, that changes things. Loan-funded refunds must go back to the lender, not to your account. Student: So I won't see the money myself? Clerk: Correct. It reduces your loan balance instead, which actually saves you interest later. Student: I hadn't thought of it that way. That's fine, then. Clerk: I'll correct the routing so the credit goes to your lender this week.

We need help with weekend tours and the front desk. Which interests you? Student: I love talking about art, so tours sound perfect. Coordinator: Tour guides go through a two-week training on the current exhibits. Are you free Saturday mornings? Student: Yes, mornings are open for me. Coordinator: Good. Training starts this Saturday at ten. After that, you'd lead one tour a week. Student: How long is each tour? Coordinator: About forty-five minutes, and you'd cover maybe six artworks. Student: Do I need an art background? Coordinator: Not at all. The training gives you a script, and you'll add your own style over time. Student: That's encouraging. Is there any reward besides experience? Coordinator: Volunteers get free entry to all special exhibitions and a reference letter after twenty hours. Student: Count me in. Where do I sign?

Teaching Assistant: Let's see your work. Walk me through your steps. Student: I used the formula for kinetic energy, then divided by time. Teaching Assistant: There's the issue. Problem five asks for power, but you mixed up two formulas. Student: Aren't energy and power the same thing? Teaching Assistant: No. Power is energy divided by time, but you divided the wrong quantity. Start from work done, then divide by time. Student: Oh, so I should use work, not just kinetic energy at one point. Teaching Assistant: Exactly. Compute the total work over the interval first. Student: Let me try. That gives a reasonable number now. Teaching Assistant: See? The method matters more than the final digit. Redo six and seven the same way. Student: I will. sets.passage: Listen to part of a lecture in a biology class. Today I want to talk about fungi that glow in the dark—a phenomenon called foxfire. Now, several dozen mushroom species produce a soft green light from their gills, and for a long time nobody knew why. The glow comes from a chemical reaction involving a compound the fungus makes, plus oxygen and an enzyme. But here's the puzzle: producing light costs energy, so it must give the fungus some advantage. Recent field experiments offer a clue. Researchers placed fake glowing mushrooms, made of resin with tiny lights inside, among real ones on the forest floor. The glowing fakes attracted far more insects—beetles, flies, ants—than dark ones. And that matters because these fungi rely on insects to carry their spores to new locations. So the light seems to be an invitation. It's essentially advertising, drawing in the very couriers the fungus needs to reproduce and spread across the forest.

questions.prompt: What is the main topic of the lecture?
options: How fungi digest wood on the forest floor
How insects navigate in dark forests
The chemical structure of fungal enzymes

questions.prompt: According to the professor, what did the researchers use in their experiment?
options: Artificial glowing mushrooms made of resin

questions.prompt: What does the professor imply about the fungi's glow?
options: It warns insects that the fungus is toxic
It is an accidental byproduct with no purpose
It helps the fungus reproduce by attracting spore carriers
It allows the fungus to absorb more oxygen

sets.explain_zh: 发光真菌用绿光吸引昆虫帮助传播孢子，实验用树脂假蘑菇证明发光是一种“广告”。

sets.passage: Listen to part of a lecture in a history class. When we picture learning a trade in medieval towns, we should think about guilds. A guild was an association of craftsmen—say, weavers or goldsmiths—who controlled who could practice their trade. To become a member, a young person began as an apprentice, usually around age twelve. The apprentice lived in the master's household, received food and lodging, but little or no pay, and in exchange learned the craft over perhaps seven years. Now, this system wasn't only about teaching skills. The guild also protected quality. By limiting the number of apprentices each master could take, guilds kept the supply of trained workers low, which supported prices and wages. After finishing, an apprentice became a journeyman, working for wages while saving to open his own shop. The final step was producing a 'masterpiece'—a sample proving his skill—to be judged by senior members. Only then could he join as a full master. So the guild shaped not just training, but the whole economic life of the craft.

questions.prompt: According to the professor, where did an apprentice typically live?
What was a 'masterpiece' in this context?
options: A sample proving a worker's skill
A famous work displayed in a town hall
A payment made to the guild
A contract signed by the master

questions.prompt: Why does the professor mention that guilds limited the number of apprentices?
options: To show guilds cared mainly about education
To explain how guilds influenced prices and wages
To suggest few people wanted to learn trades
To prove that masters were poorly paid

sets.explain_zh: 行会通过限制学徒数量压低劳动力供给以支撑价格和工资，学徒制塑造了整个行业的经济生活。

sets.passage: Listen to part of a lecture in a psychology class. Let's talk about how the timing of study affects memory. Suppose you have four hours to prepare for an exam. You could study all four hours in one sitting—that's called massed practice, or cramming. Or you could break those four hours into shorter sessions spread across several days. Decades of research point to a clear winner: spreading it out. This is known as the spacing effect. When you space your study, you tend to remember more, and for longer, than when you cram the same total amount. Why? One idea is that each time you return to the material after a gap, your brain has partly forgotten it, so retrieving it again requires effort—and that effort strengthens the memory. Cramming, by contrast, feels productive because the information stays fresh in the moment, but that fluency is misleading. It fades quickly. So the uncomfortable truth is that the study method that feels easiest often teaches you the least.

questions.prompt: What is 'massed practice'?
options: Studying in short sessions over many days
Studying all at once in a single sitting

questions.prompt: According to the professor, what strengthens memory during spaced study?
options: The effort of retrieving partly forgotten material

questions.prompt: What does the professor mean by saying the easiest method 'often teaches you the least'?
options: Cramming feels effective but produces weaker memory
Students should avoid studying before exams
Spacing requires more total hours than cramming

sets.explain_zh: 分散学习（间隔效应）比集中突击记得更牢，因为间隔后回忆需努力，而突击带来的流畅感具有误导性。

sets.passage: Listen to part of a lecture in an environmental science class. Have you noticed that a city can feel several degrees warmer than the countryside just beyond it? This is the urban heat island effect. The main cause is materials. Asphalt roads and dark rooftops absorb sunlight during the day and store that heat, then release it slowly at night. Meanwhile, cities have removed much of the vegetation that would otherwise cool the air. Trees and grass release water vapor, which lowers temperatures, much like sweating cools your skin. Replace them with concrete, and that cooling disappears. Add waste heat from air conditioners and vehicles, and the difference grows. Why should we care? Higher city temperatures raise energy demand for cooling, worsen air pollution, and can be dangerous during heat waves, especially for the elderly. The good news is that solutions exist. Planting trees, installing 'green roofs' covered with plants, and using lighter-colored, reflective surfaces can all bring city temperatures down noticeably. Design choices, in other words, directly shape how hot a neighborhood becomes.

questions.prompt: What is the lecture mainly about?
options: Why cities are warmer than surrounding areas
The health effects of air pollution

questions.prompt: How does vegetation help cool the air, according to the professor?
options: By blocking sunlight with tall trunks
By absorbing heat into the soil
By releasing water vapor into the air
By reflecting light back to the sky

questions.prompt: Why does the professor mention green roofs and reflective surfaces?
options: To show the problem cannot be solved
To give examples of ways to reduce the effect

sets.explain_zh: 城市因深色建材吸热、植被减少而比郊区热，教授列举种树、绿色屋顶和反光表面等降温办法。

sets.passage: Listen to part of a lecture in an art history class. When we admire a wall painting from the Renaissance, it's worth asking how it was actually made. There were two main techniques. The first, buon fresco, means 'true fresco.' The artist spread a layer of wet plaster on the wall and painted directly into it with pigments mixed in water. As the plaster dried, a chemical reaction sealed the color into the wall itself. The result is remarkably durable—the paint becomes part of the surface. But there's a catch: the artist had to work fast, finishing a section before the plaster dried, with no chance to correct mistakes. The second method, fresco secco, means 'dry fresco.' Here the artist painted onto plaster that had already dried, using pigment mixed with a binder like egg. This was more forgiving and allowed finer detail, but the paint sat on the surface rather than bonding with it. Over centuries, secco painting tends to flake and fade. That's why the best-preserved wall paintings are usually true frescoes.

questions.prompt: In buon fresco, what does the artist paint onto?
options: Dry plaster with an egg binder

questions.prompt: According to the professor, what is a disadvantage of buon fresco?
options: The colors fade within a few years
The artist must work quickly with no corrections
It can only be used for small paintings

questions.prompt: Why are true frescoes usually better preserved than secco paintings?
options: They were painted by more skilled artists
They were kept indoors more often
The pigment bonds into the wall itself

sets.explain_zh: 湿壁画颜料随灰泥干燥融入墙体而耐久，但须快速作画不能修改；干壁画易脱落，故保存最好的多为湿壁画。

sets.passage: Listen to part of a lecture in an astronomy class. One of the oldest problems in astronomy is simply this: how far away are the stars? For nearby stars, we use a clever method called parallax. Hold up your thumb at arm's length and look at it with one eye, then the other—your thumb appears to shift against the background. The closer the object, the bigger the shift. Astronomers do the same thing with stars, but their two 'eyes' are the Earth's position on opposite sides of its orbit, six months apart. As Earth moves, a nearby star appears to shift slightly against the far more distant background stars. Measure that tiny angle, and geometry gives you the distance. The catch is that even for the nearest stars, the angle is incredibly small—far smaller than anything the eye can detect. It took precise instruments, and later telescopes in space, to measure it reliably. Parallax only works out to a certain range, but within that range, it gives us direct, trustworthy distances—the foundation for measuring the rest of the universe.

questions.prompt: In the parallax method, what serves as the two 'eyes'?
options: Earth's position at opposite sides of its orbit

questions.prompt: According to the professor, why is stellar parallax hard to measure?
options: The shift angle is extremely small
Stars are constantly moving on their own
Clouds block the view for months

questions.prompt: Why does the professor describe holding up a thumb?
options: To show that the eye cannot judge distance
To illustrate the parallax principle with a simple example
To prove that stars are closer than they appear
To explain why humans have two eyes

sets.explain_zh: 视差法以地球公转轨道两端为“双眼”测量近星相对遥远背景的微小位移，角度极小，教授用大拇指例子说明原理。

sets.passage: Listen to part of a lecture in an economics class. Imagine a village with a shared pasture open to everyone. Each herder can graze as many animals as they like. Now, from the point of view of a single herder, adding one more cow is a good deal: that herder gets all the benefit of the extra animal, while the cost—slightly less grass for everyone—is shared across the whole village. So each herder, acting sensibly for themselves, keeps adding animals. The problem is that if everyone follows the same logic, the pasture is soon stripped bare, and everyone loses. This is the tragedy of the commons. It appears whenever a shared resource has no limits on use—think of overfished oceans or polluted air. The key insight is that individual self-interest and the group's interest pull in opposite directions. How do we escape it? Communities have found answers: setting rules about how much each person may take, assigning ownership, or charging fees for use. Without some such arrangement, a freely shared resource tends to be used up.

questions.prompt: Why does each herder keep adding animals to the pasture?
options: The grass grows faster with more animals
They receive the full benefit while sharing the cost
Other herders pay them to do so

questions.prompt: Which of these does the professor give as an example of the tragedy of the commons?
What is the key insight the professor emphasizes?
options: Individual and group interests can conflict
Shared resources are always more efficient
People rarely act in their own interest

sets.explain_zh: 公地悲剧指个人从共享资源多取的收益归己、成本却由众人分摊，结果资源被耗尽，反映个人与集体利益的冲突。

sets.passage: Listen to part of a lecture in a geology class. Some lakes keep a natural calendar at the bottom, and geologists have learned to read it. In certain lakes, especially those fed by melting glaciers, the sediment settles in distinct yearly layers called varves. Here's how it works. In spring and summer, fast-flowing meltwater carries coarse, light-colored sand and silt into the lake. In winter, the lake surface freezes over, the water grows still, and fine, dark clay slowly settles out. So each year produces a pair of layers—one light, one dark. Count the pairs, and you can count the years, much like counting tree rings. But varves tell us more than age. A thick summer layer suggests a warm year with heavy melting; a thin one suggests a cool year. So by measuring layer thickness, researchers reconstruct past climate stretching back thousands of years. The main limitation is that clear varves form only in special conditions—still, deep lakes without burrowing animals to stir the mud. Where they exist, though, they're an extraordinarily precise record of time.

questions.prompt: What produces the light-colored layer in a varve?
options: Winter clay settling in still water
Spring and summer meltwater carrying sand and silt

questions.prompt: What can a thick summer layer indicate?
options: A cold year with little melting
A year with heavy rainfall only
A warm year with heavy melting
A year when the lake dried up

questions.prompt: Why does the professor compare varves to tree rings?
options: Both are destroyed by burrowing animals
Both can be counted to determine age

sets.passage: Listen to part of a lecture in a linguistics class. Why is it that a young child can pick up a language almost effortlessly, while an adult often struggles for years and still speaks with an accent? Many linguists point to what's called the critical period. The idea is that there's a window early in life—roughly up to puberty—when the brain is especially prepared to absorb language. During this window, children learn grammar and pronunciation naturally, just from being surrounded by speech. After it closes, learning a language becomes a more deliberate, effortful task. What's the evidence? Consider studies of people learning a second language at different ages. Those who begin in early childhood typically reach native-like fluency, while those who start as adults rarely do, no matter how hard they work, especially with pronunciation. There are also rare, tragic cases of children isolated from language altogether; when discovered late, they struggle to acquire normal grammar. Now, adults do have advantages—they learn vocabulary faster at first. But for effortless, complete mastery, earlier really does seem to be better.

questions.prompt: What is the critical period?
options: An early window when the brain readily absorbs language
The age when children first learn to read
A period when adults learn vocabulary fastest
The time it takes to master pronunciation

questions.prompt: According to the professor, what advantage do adults have?
options: They learn vocabulary faster at first
They never speak with an accent

questions.prompt: Why does the professor mention children isolated from language?
options: To show adults cannot learn languages
To argue children should start school earlier
To provide evidence that late exposure impairs language learning

sets.explain_zh: 关键期假说认为青春期前大脑最易自然习得语言，二语研究和语言隔离儿童的案例都支持晚接触会削弱语言习得。

sets.passage: Listen to part of a lecture in a chemistry class. Living cells run thousands of chemical reactions, and most of them, left alone, would happen far too slowly to keep you alive. The solution is a catalyst—a substance that speeds up a reaction without being used up itself. In cells, these catalysts are proteins called enzymes. Here's the basic idea. Every reaction has to climb over an energy barrier before it can proceed, like pushing a boulder over a hill. An enzyme works by lowering that barrier. It doesn't change how much energy the reaction releases overall; it just provides an easier path. It does this by grabbing hold of the reacting molecules and holding them in exactly the right position to react. Because the enzyme itself emerges unchanged, a single enzyme molecule can perform the same trick over and over, thousands of times a second. One more crucial point: enzymes are highly specific. Each one is shaped to fit only certain molecules, like a lock and key. That specificity lets the cell control which reactions speed up and which don't—a kind of chemical management system.

questions.prompt: What is a catalyst?
options: A molecule destroyed during the reaction
A substance that speeds a reaction without being used up

questions.prompt: How does an enzyme speed up a reaction?
options: By raising the temperature of the cell
By becoming part of the product

questions.prompt: Why does the professor mention a lock and key?
options: To show enzymes are easily broken
To explain that enzymes act only on specific molecules
To prove enzymes are used up quickly
To argue that all enzymes are identical

sets.explain_zh: 酶是不被消耗的蛋白质催化剂，通过降低能垒加速反应，且像锁与钥匙般专一，只作用于特定分子，从而调控细胞反应。

sets.passage: Listen to part of a lecture in a biology class. Today let's look at how honeybees tell one another where to find food. When a scout bee discovers a rich patch of flowers, it flies back to the hive and performs what we call a waggle dance. It runs in a straight line while shaking its body, then loops back and repeats the run. Here's the remarkable part: the angle of that straight run, measured against vertical, signals the direction of the food relative to the sun. And the longer the waggle run lasts, the farther away the food is. So in a completely dark hive, using only movement, one bee conveys both direction and distance. Researchers confirmed this by training bees to visit feeders, then decoding their dances to predict where they would send their hivemates — and the predictions matched the feeders. It's one of the few known cases of an animal using a kind of symbolic code.

questions.prompt: What is the lecture mainly about?
options: How honeybees communicate the location of food
Why bees are attracted to bright colors
How bees survive the winter months

questions.prompt: According to the professor, what does the length of the waggle run indicate?
Why does the professor mention that researchers decoded the dances to predict feeder locations?
options: To show that the dance really carries direction and distance information
To explain why bees sometimes get lost
To argue that bees cannot see the sun
To describe how honey is harvested
