sets.passage: Listen to part of a lecture in a botany class. When we look at a forest, we see separate trees standing apart. But underground, the picture is very different. The roots of most plants are wrapped in threadlike fungi, forming a partnership called a mycorrhiza. The fungus extends far into the soil, reaching water and minerals the roots can't get on their own, and passes these to the plant. In return, the plant supplies the fungus with sugars made through photosynthesis. It's a fair trade. But here's the striking part. A single fungus can connect to many plants at once, linking them into a shared network. Through these fungal threads, trees can actually transfer resources to one another. A large tree in sunlight may send sugar to a shaded seedling struggling below. Some researchers even suggest the network carries chemical warning signals when insects attack. Now, we should be careful not to romanticize this as trees being 'friends.' The fungus is acting in its own interest too. Still, it means a forest is less a crowd of individuals and more a connected community.

questions.prompt: What does the fungus provide to the plant?
options: Water and minerals from the soil

questions.prompt: What can pass between trees through the network?
Why does the professor warn against calling trees 'friends'?
options: Because trees cannot communicate at all
Because the network does not really exist
Because the fungus also acts in its own interest

sets.explain_zh: 菌根网络中真菌为植物输送水和矿物、换取糖分，还能在树木间转移资源甚至传递警示信号，但教授提醒真菌亦为自身利益，勿过度拟人化。

sets.passage: Listen to part of a lecture in a physics class. Every object has a natural frequency—a rate at which it 'likes' to vibrate. Pluck a guitar string, tap a wine glass, and you hear that frequency as a tone. Now, something interesting happens when you push an object at exactly its natural frequency. Each push arrives in step with the object's own motion, adding a little energy each time. The vibrations grow larger and larger. This build-up is called resonance. You've felt it on a playground swing: small pushes, perfectly timed, send the swing higher and higher. Resonance can be useful—it's how musical instruments amplify sound. But it can also be dangerous. If wind or an earthquake shakes a bridge or a building at its natural frequency, the structure can vibrate so violently that it tears itself apart. That's why engineers carefully calculate these frequencies and add features that absorb energy, breaking up the dangerous rhythm. So understanding resonance isn't just theory—it's the difference between a structure that sways safely and one that collapses.

questions.prompt: What is resonance?
options: Vibrations growing larger when an object is pushed at its natural frequency
The tone an object makes when struck once
The frequency at which sound disappears
The energy an object loses over time

questions.prompt: What does the playground swing example illustrate?
options: How swings lose energy to friction
How timed pushes build up motion
Why swings have a fixed length

questions.prompt: Why do engineers calculate the natural frequencies of structures?
options: To make bridges produce musical tones
To increase a building's natural frequency
To prevent structures from vibrating destructively
To measure the speed of the wind

sets.explain_zh: 共振指以物体固有频率施力使振动不断增大，如荡秋千；它能放大乐音，也能使桥梁建筑剧烈振动而毁坏，故工程师须计算固有频率并加以抑制。

sets.passage: Listen to part of a lecture in an anthropology class. In many societies, giving a gift is far more than a kind gesture—it's a social obligation woven into the fabric of community. Anthropologists have long studied systems of exchange where gifts flow back and forth between groups. The key principle is reciprocity: when you receive a gift, you are expected, sooner or later, to give one in return. This isn't a cold transaction like buying bread. The delay matters. If you gave something back instantly, of equal value, you'd be treating it like a sale, even insulting the giver. Instead, the gap in time creates an ongoing bond—an unpaid debt that keeps two parties connected and obliged to each other. Over years, these chains of giving build alliances, mark status, and hold communities together. A generous giver gains prestige; someone who receives but never reciprocates loses respect. So when we study these exchanges, we're really studying how societies create trust and cooperation without money, contracts, or courts. The gift, in a sense, is the glue.

questions.prompt: What is reciprocity?
options: The practice of buying goods with money
The habit of refusing all gifts
Giving a gift only to relatives
The expectation to give a gift in return

questions.prompt: Why does giving something back instantly cause a problem?
options: It treats the gift like a sale and can insult the giver
It costs more money than waiting

questions.prompt: What main point does the professor make about gift exchange?
options: Gifts are less important than trade
Gifts create trust and bind communities without money or contracts
Only wealthy people can give gifts

sets.explain_zh: 互惠原则要求受礼者日后回礼，延迟回礼形成持续的社会纽带；这种赠礼体系在没有货币和契约时建立信任、凝聚社会。

sets.passage: Listen to part of a lecture in an archaeology class. Wood can be a surprisingly precise clock, and the method that reads it is called dendrochronology—dating by tree rings. Each year, a tree adds a new ring of growth just under its bark. In a good year, with plenty of rain, the ring is wide; in a dry year, it's narrow. So a cross-section of a trunk shows a sequence of wide and narrow rings, a pattern that reflects the local climate over the tree's life. Here's the clever part. Because all trees in a region experience the same weather, they share the same pattern of good and bad years. By matching the ring patterns of living trees to those in older, dead wood—say, a beam from an ancient house—researchers can extend the record back thousands of years and pin a piece of wood to an exact calendar year. This gives archaeologists something rare: a precise date for when a timber was cut. It also, as a bonus, records droughts and cold spells from long before written history.

questions.prompt: What produces a wide tree ring?
options: A year with plenty of rain

questions.prompt: How is the ring record extended back in time?
options: By counting rings on a single old tree
By measuring the height of trees
By matching patterns of living trees to older dead wood

questions.prompt: What does dendrochronology give archaeologists?
options: A precise date for when a timber was cut
The exact height of an ancient forest
The species of every tree used
The number of people in a village

sets.explain_zh: 树木年轮宽窄反映当年降水，同区域树木图案一致；通过将活树与古木年轮图案匹配可延伸年表数千年，为木材砍伐年份提供精确定年。

sets.passage: Listen to part of a lecture in a meteorology class. High above our heads, at the altitude where airplanes cruise, rivers of air race around the planet at tremendous speed. These are the jet streams. What creates them? It comes down to temperature differences. Near the equator, air is warm; near the poles, it's cold. Where these two air masses meet, the sharp contrast in temperature generates a strong, fast-flowing current of wind high in the atmosphere. Now, jet streams don't flow in straight lines. They snake north and south in great waves, and those waves largely determine our day-to-day weather. When a jet stream dips south, it can drag cold polar air down over a region; when it bulges north, warm air pushes up. Storms tend to form and travel along the jet stream, so forecasters watch it closely. Airlines watch it too—flying east with the jet stream at your back can save fuel and time, while flying against it slows you down. So this invisible high-altitude wind shapes both our weather and our travel.

questions.prompt: What creates jet streams?
options: Temperature differences between warm and cold air

questions.prompt: What happens when a jet stream dips south?
options: It stops all storms from forming
It can bring cold polar air over a region

questions.prompt: Why does the professor mention airlines?
options: To argue that flying is dangerous
To prove jet streams are slowing down
To show the jet stream affects travel, not just weather

sets.explain_zh: 急流由赤道与极地间温差在高空形成，其南北弯曲决定日常天气并引导风暴，还影响航班顺逆风的耗时耗油。

sets.passage: Listen to part of a lecture in a paleontology class. Most fossils are made of stone—the original tissue long since replaced by minerals. But there's a rarer, more astonishing kind of fossil that preserves ancient life almost intact, and it comes from tree resin. Millions of years ago, certain trees oozed sticky resin. An insect crawling on the bark could get trapped, coated, and completely engulfed. Over time, the resin hardened, and after millions of years underground it turned into amber—a clear, golden solid. What makes amber special is that it seals its captive away from air and water, the two things that normally cause decay. So inside a piece of amber, we can find a fly, a spider, even a feather, preserved in three dimensions, down to the tiny hairs on its legs. Some specimens are so detailed you'd think the creature was trapped yesterday. This gives scientists a uniquely vivid window into ancient ecosystems—not a flattened imprint in rock, but the actual organism. Of course, amber only captures small things that resin could engulf, so it gives us just one slice of the ancient world.

questions.prompt: What is amber?
Why do organisms preserve so well in amber?
options: It seals them away from air and water
It replaces their tissue with minerals

questions.prompt: What limits the kinds of life amber can preserve?
options: It captures only plants, never animals
It captures only creatures that lived in water
It captures only small organisms that resin could engulf
It captures only animals with bones

sets.explain_zh: 昆虫被树脂包裹并硬化成琥珀，隔绝空气和水而立体保存细节，为古生态提供生动窗口，但只能保存树脂能包住的小型生物。

sets.passage: Listen to part of a lecture in a sociology class. When people look for a new job, where do the best leads come from? You might guess close friends and family—the people who know you best and want to help most. But research suggests something surprising. Often, the most useful information comes not from close friends but from acquaintances—people you know only slightly. Sociologists call this the strength of weak ties. Here's the logic. Your close friends tend to move in the same circles you do. They know the same people, hear the same news, so they rarely tell you anything you don't already know. An acquaintance, by contrast, lives in a different social world. They're connected to jobs, opportunities, and information that never reach your inner circle. So a weak tie acts like a bridge to new territory. This doesn't mean close friends don't matter—they offer support and trust that acquaintances can't. But when it comes to spreading fresh information across a society, the loose, casual connections often do the heavy lifting. It's a nice reminder that even distant relationships have real value.

questions.prompt: What are 'weak ties'?
Why do close friends rarely provide new information?
options: They move in the same circles and know the same things
They do not want to help you

questions.prompt: What main point does the professor make about weak ties?
options: Close friends are useless for finding jobs
Loose connections often spread fresh information across society
People should avoid making close friends

sets.explain_zh: “弱联系的力量”指点头之交身处不同社交圈，能带来密友不知道的新信息和机会，像桥梁般在社会中传播资讯。

sets.passage: Listen to part of a lecture in a marine biology class. A coral reef looks like rock, but it's built by living animals—tiny creatures called polyps. And those polyps have a secret partner. Living inside their tissues are microscopic algae. Through photosynthesis, the algae produce food and share most of it with the coral; in return, the coral gives them shelter and nutrients. The algae also give coral its brilliant color. This partnership is the engine of the whole reef. But it's fragile. When the water grows too warm—even just a couple of degrees above normal for a few weeks—the relationship breaks down. Stressed, the coral expels the algae living inside it. Without them, the coral turns ghostly white; we call this coral bleaching. And here's the danger: a bleached coral isn't dead, but it's starving, because it has lost its main food source. If cool conditions return quickly, the algae can come back and the coral recovers. But if the heat lasts too long, the coral dies—and with it, the reef that shelters a quarter of all marine species.

questions.prompt: What lives inside coral polyps?
What causes coral bleaching?
Why is a bleached coral in danger?
options: It has lost its main food source and is starving
It can never produce color again
It is eaten by the algae

sets.explain_zh: 珊瑚虫体内共生的微藻通过光合作用供养珊瑚并赋予色彩；水温过高时珊瑚排出微藻而白化，虽未死却因失去主要食物来源而挨饿，持续高温会致其死亡。

sets.passage: Listen to part of a lecture in a neuroscience class. We often think of sleep as simply shutting down for the night. But your brain is remarkably busy while you sleep, and one of its key jobs is managing memory. During the day, your experiences are recorded in a temporary, fragile form. If nothing further happened, many would quickly fade. What sleep does is consolidate them—move important memories into more stable, long-term storage. Studies show this clearly. Learn a list of words, then sleep, and you'll recall more the next day than someone who stayed awake the same number of hours. It seems the sleeping brain replays the day's events, strengthening the connections that store them. Different stages of sleep matter for different things. Deep sleep appears especially important for facts and events, while dream-rich sleep seems to help with skills and emotional memories. The practical lesson is striking: pulling an all-nighter before an exam may actually backfire. Without sleep, the very learning you worked so hard for never gets properly filed away. Sleep, in other words, isn't lost time—it's when learning becomes permanent.

questions.prompt: What does the brain do with memories during sleep?
options: It erases the unimportant ones only
It consolidates them into long-term storage
It records new experiences for the first time
It keeps them in a fragile temporary form

questions.prompt: What is deep sleep especially important for?
Why does the professor mention pulling an all-nighter?
options: To recommend studying only at night
To show that exams are unfair
To prove sleep is a waste of time
To warn that losing sleep can undermine learning

sets.explain_zh: 睡眠把白天脆弱的记忆巩固为长期记忆，深睡有助事实记忆、梦境睡眠有助技能与情绪记忆；故考前熬夜反而妨碍学习成果的固化。

sets.passage: Listen to part of a lecture in an art history class. Imagine a painting that, up close, looks like nothing but thousands of tiny separate dots of pure color. Step back, and those dots blend into a shimmering, complete image. This technique is called pointillism, and it rests on a clever idea about how vision works. Instead of mixing paints on a palette, the artist places small dots of unmixed color side by side—say, dots of pure blue next to dots of pure yellow. The mixing happens not on the canvas but in your eye. From a distance, your visual system blends the neighboring dots, and you perceive green. Why bother? The artists believed that color mixed by the eye stays more vivid and luminous than color muddied together on a palette. There's some truth to that. The cost, though, is enormous labor—covering a large canvas one dot at a time takes extraordinary patience. And the effect only works at the right viewing distance. Too close, and the image dissolves into specks; too far, and the detail is lost. It's a technique that literally depends on where the viewer stands.

questions.prompt: In pointillism, where does the color mixing happen?
Why did artists use this technique?
options: It was faster than normal painting
They believed eye-mixed color stays more vivid

questions.prompt: What is a drawback of pointillism?
options: It only works at the right viewing distance
It cannot use blue and yellow

sets.explain_zh: 点彩派把纯色小点并置，让观者的眼睛在远处自行混合成色，据信比调色板混色更鲜亮，但耗工巨大且只在特定观看距离才有效。

sets.passage: Listen to part of a lecture in an ecology class. Not all species matter equally to an ecosystem. Some can disappear with little effect, while others, if removed, cause the whole system to unravel. We call this second kind a keystone species—named after the central stone in an arch that holds all the others in place. Take a classic coastal example. Sea otters eat sea urchins. Sea urchins, in turn, eat kelp—the tall underwater plants that form rich forests along the shore. Where otters thrive, they keep urchin numbers in check, and vast kelp forests flourish, sheltering countless fish and other creatures. But remove the otters, and the urchins explode in number. They devour the kelp, and the forest collapses into a barren zone with little life. Notice that the otter isn't the most abundant animal here, nor the largest. Its importance comes from its position in the food web—its effect ripples outward far beyond its own numbers. That's the essence of a keystone species: identify it, protect it, and you protect an entire community that depends on it.

questions.prompt: What do sea otters eat?
What happens when otters are removed from the ecosystem?
options: Urchins multiply and destroy the kelp forest

questions.prompt: Why is the otter called a keystone species?
options: It is the largest animal in the sea
Its effect on the ecosystem far exceeds its numbers
It is the most abundant species there

sets.explain_zh: 关键种如同拱心石，海獭数量虽不多，却通过捕食海胆维持海藻林；一旦移除，海胆暴增吃光海藻，整个群落崩溃，其影响远超其数量。

sets.passage: Listen to part of a lecture in an economics class. Traditional economics assumes people are rational calculators, weighing costs and benefits coolly. But real human behavior often departs from that ideal in predictable ways. Consider a striking pattern called loss aversion. Experiments show that the pain of losing something feels much stronger than the pleasure of gaining the same thing. Losing twenty dollars hurts more than finding twenty dollars feels good—roughly twice as much, studies suggest. Think about what that implies. People will often pass up a fair bet, say a coin flip to win or lose the same amount, because the possible loss looms larger than the equal possible gain. Loss aversion also explains why we cling to things we own. Once something is 'mine,' giving it up registers as a loss, so we demand more to sell it than we'd have paid to buy it. Marketers know this well—'don't miss out' is a more powerful message than 'here's a benefit.' The lesson for economists is humbling: to predict real choices, we can't assume people treat gains and losses as mirror images. They simply don't.

questions.prompt: What is loss aversion?
options: People enjoy gains more than they fear losses
People treat gains and losses equally
The pain of a loss feels stronger than the pleasure of an equal gain

questions.prompt: Why do people often pass up a fair coin-flip bet?
options: They expect to lose every time
The possible loss looms larger than the equal gain

questions.prompt: Why does the professor mention marketers and 'don't miss out'?
options: To show that loss aversion appears in everyday persuasion
To prove that marketing never works
To argue that people should not shop

sets.explain_zh: 损失厌恶指失去某物的痛苦约为获得等量之物快乐的两倍，导致人们回避公平赌局、惜售所拥之物，营销也常用“别错过”来利用这一心理。

sets.passage: Listen to part of a lecture in a geology class. A century ago, the idea that continents move was ridiculed. The obvious objection was: how could solid continents plow through solid ocean floor? The answer came from the ocean floor itself. Running down the middle of the Atlantic is an underwater mountain range, a ridge. Along its crest, molten rock rises from below, cools, and forms brand-new ocean floor. As more rises, it pushes the older floor outward to either side, like a conveyor belt. This is seafloor spreading. The continents don't plow through anything; they ride along on top as the floor moves. The proof was elegant. The rock nearest the ridge is youngest, and it gets progressively older the farther you go from the ridge on both sides—a mirror image. And the pattern of magnetism frozen into the rock matches on either side too, recording the same history. Once this was understood, the whole theory of plate tectonics fell into place. The Earth's surface, we realized, is broken into moving plates, and the ocean floors are constantly being created and, elsewhere, destroyed.

questions.prompt: What happens at the mid-ocean ridge?
options: Old ocean floor sinks and melts
Molten rock rises and forms new ocean floor

questions.prompt: Where is the youngest rock found?
Why does the professor mention the objection about continents 'plowing through' the floor?
options: To prove continents cannot move at all
To argue the ridge does not exist
To show how seafloor spreading resolved that objection

sets.explain_zh: 海底扩张说明洋中脊处岩浆上涌形成新洋底并把旧洋底向两侧推移，大陆是搭载其上而非“犁过”洋底；离脊越远岩石越老、磁性对称，由此确立板块构造理论。

sets.passage: Listen to part of a lecture in a history class. Before the printing press, every book in Europe was copied by hand, usually by scribes working for months on a single volume. Books were therefore rare and enormously expensive—a luxury owned by the wealthy, the church, and universities. Most people never held one. Then, in the fifteenth century, movable-type printing changed everything. A printer could set the letters of a page once and then run off hundreds of identical copies quickly. The cost of a book plummeted. Within decades, the number of books in Europe multiplied enormously. Now, why does this matter beyond convenience? Cheaper books meant more people could own them, which created a reason to learn to read—and reading spread. Ideas, once locked in a few handwritten volumes, could now travel fast and far. New thoughts in science, religion, and politics reached wide audiences almost overnight, and they were much harder for any authority to suppress. Historians often argue that this single technology helped set off waves of change that reshaped European society. Information, quite suddenly, had been set loose.

questions.prompt: How were books made before the printing press?
What did movable-type printing allow?
Why does the professor say information 'had been set loose'?
options: Books became more expensive than before
Scribes were paid more for their work
Ideas could spread widely and were hard to suppress

sets.explain_zh: 印刷术前书籍靠手抄、昂贵稀少；活字印刷使成本骤降、书籍激增，促使更多人识字，思想得以广泛快速传播且难被压制，深刻改变了欧洲社会。

sets.passage: Listen to part of a lecture in a psychology class. Here's a troubling question: if you collapsed on a busy street, would a large crowd make it more likely, or less likely, that someone helps you? Common sense says more people, more help. But research points the other way. Psychologists call it the bystander effect: the more people who witness an emergency, the less likely any single one of them is to step in. Why? Part of the answer is a phenomenon called diffusion of responsibility. When you're the only witness, the responsibility to act rests entirely on you. But in a crowd, that responsibility feels spread out—everyone assumes someone else will handle it, so no one does. There's also social influence: people look to others for cues, and if everyone is standing calmly, each person concludes it must not be a real emergency. Understanding this has a practical payoff. If you ever need help in a crowd, don't just call out to everyone. Point to one specific person and ask them directly. By doing so, you place the responsibility squarely on that individual—and break the spell.

questions.prompt: What is the bystander effect?
options: Crowds always help faster than individuals
More witnesses make any one person less likely to help

questions.prompt: What is diffusion of responsibility?
options: Responsibility feels spread across the crowd so no one acts
Everyone rushes to help at once
One leader takes charge of the group

questions.prompt: Why does the professor advise pointing to one specific person?
options: To make the crowd feel guilty
To place responsibility on a specific individual and overcome the effect
To prove that crowds are dangerous
To show that no one ever helps

sets.explain_zh: 旁观者效应指目击者越多、单个人越不愿相助，原因包括责任分散和社会参照；故求助时应指定某一个人，把责任明确落到其身上以打破僵局。

sets.passage: Listen to part of a lecture in an astronomy class. A comet spends most of its life as a dark, frozen lump wandering the outer solar system—essentially a 'dirty snowball' of ice and dust, just a few kilometers across. For most of its orbit, it's invisible to us. But something dramatic happens when its long path brings it close to the Sun. The Sun's heat warms the ice, which turns directly into gas, releasing trapped dust. This forms a glowing cloud around the comet, and then the famous tail. Here's a detail people often get wrong: a comet's tail does not stream out behind it like smoke behind a train. Instead, the tail always points away from the Sun, pushed outward by sunlight and the flow of particles streaming from the Sun. So as a comet swings around and heads back out, its tail actually leads the way, pointing ahead of it. Once the comet retreats from the Sun and cools, the tail vanishes, and it becomes a dark lump again, waiting years or centuries for its next pass. The show, in other words, is temporary.

questions.prompt: What is a comet for most of its orbit?
options: A dark, frozen lump of ice and dust

questions.prompt: In which direction does a comet's tail point?
Why does the professor mention 'smoke behind a train'?
options: To correct a common misconception about the tail's direction
To explain how trains produce smoke
To show comets move as fast as trains
To prove the tail is made of smoke

sets.explain_zh: 彗星平时是冰尘构成的暗冻块，近日时冰升华成气释出尘埃形成彗尾；教授纠正误解——彗尾并非拖在身后，而是被阳光和太阳粒子推得始终背向太阳。

sets.passage: Listen to part of a lecture in an environmental science class. We usually think of animals as adapting to their environment. But a few species reshape it so profoundly that they engineer whole landscapes. The beaver is the classic case. A beaver fells trees and piles branches and mud across a stream, building a dam. Behind that dam, the water backs up and spreads out, turning a narrow, fast creek into a broad, calm pond and wetland. Consider what that one structure does. The pond slows the water, so sediment settles out instead of rushing downstream, and the water leaving is cleaner. The wetland stores water, releasing it slowly, which helps a landscape stay moist through dry spells and buffers floods after storms. And the new habitat draws in fish, birds, insects, and amphibians that couldn't live along a fast, narrow stream. In a real sense, the beaver creates an ecosystem that many other species depend on. This is why, when beavers vanish from a region, streams often grow drier and less diverse—and why restoring beavers has become a surprising tool for healing damaged watersheds.

questions.prompt: What happens to the water behind a beaver dam?
options: It backs up and spreads into a pond and wetland

questions.prompt: What happens to sediment in the beaver pond?
options: It settles out, leaving cleaner water

questions.prompt: Why does the professor call the beaver an 'engineer'?
options: It builds the strongest dams of any animal
It works harder than other animals
It uses tools like humans do
It reshapes the landscape and creates habitat for other species

sets.explain_zh: 河狸筑坝使溪流蓄成池塘湿地，减缓水流让泥沙沉降、净化并储水缓洪，还为众多物种创造栖息地，是重塑地貌的“生态工程师”。

sets.passage: Listen to part of a lecture in a biology class. Every year, some birds fly thousands of kilometers between their summer and winter homes, often returning to the very same tree. How do they find their way across oceans and continents, sometimes at night, with no landmarks? Birds use several cues together. On clear days, they navigate by the position of the Sun; on clear nights, by the patterns of the stars. But what about cloudy skies, when neither is visible? Here's the remarkable part: many birds can sense the Earth's magnetic field. The planet acts like a giant magnet, and its field varies in a regular way across the globe—so it can serve as both a compass, giving direction, and a kind of map, hinting at location. Exactly how birds detect it is still debated; some evidence points to specialized cells, other evidence to light-sensitive chemistry in the eye. What's clear from experiments is that when researchers alter the magnetic field around migrating birds, the birds change direction accordingly. So these travelers carry, in effect, a built-in navigation system we're only beginning to understand.

questions.prompt: How do birds navigate on clear nights?
options: By the patterns of the stars
By the position of the Sun

questions.prompt: What can the Earth's magnetic field provide to birds?
options: Both direction and a sense of location

questions.prompt: What does the experiment altering the magnetic field show?
options: Birds prefer to fly at night
Birds actually use the magnetic field to orient
Birds ignore the Sun and stars

sets.passage: Listen to part of a lecture in an art history class. Look at a painting from around four hundred years ago in which a single figure emerges from deep shadow, lit as if by one dramatic beam of light. That striking contrast between light and dark has a name: chiaroscuro, from the Italian words for 'light' and 'dark.' Before this technique matured, many painters lit their scenes evenly and flatly, so figures looked somewhat two-dimensional. Chiaroscuro changed that. By modeling forms with strong gradations from bright highlight to deep shadow, artists made objects appear round and solid, as if you could reach out and touch them. But chiaroscuro is more than a trick for creating volume. Artists used it to direct your attention—your eye goes straight to the brightly lit face and ignores the darkness around it. And it sets a mood. A figure surrounded by shadow can feel mysterious, tense, even spiritual. So when we analyze these paintings, we shouldn't ask only 'how is the light made?' but 'what is the darkness doing?' The shadow, handled well, is as expressive as the light itself.

options: To argue that dark paintings are unfinished

To claim that light is unimportant
To show that the technique failed

sets.explain_zh: 书店省钱选项公告，考查租书价格、逾期未还后果及提及图书馆预留本的用意（免费替代）。

sets.passage: Listen to an announcement about residence hall move-in. Hello, incoming residents, and welcome to your new home. Move-in day is this Saturday, and to keep traffic flowing smoothly, we've assigned arrival windows based on your building. Please check your email for your specific two-hour slot and try to arrive within it; showing up hours early only creates long lines in the parking area. When you arrive, pull up to the loading zone, unload quickly, and then move your vehicle to the remote lot so the next family can pull in. Volunteers with large rolling carts will be stationed at every entrance to help you carry belongings upstairs. Your room key and mailbox combination can be picked up at the check-in table in your building's lobby, where you'll need to show a photo identification. One reminder about prohibited items: candles, space heaters, and cooking appliances with open coils are not allowed in the rooms for fire-safety reasons, and they will be confiscated during inspections. If you've forgotten anything, the campus store stocks the essentials. We're thrilled to have you here.

questions.prompt: How are move-in arrival windows assigned?
Which items are prohibited in the rooms?
options: Candles, space heaters, and open-coil appliances

questions.prompt: Why does the speaker ask students to move their vehicles to the remote lot quickly?
options: So the next family can pull in
So students can reach their rooms faster

sets.explain_zh: 宿舍入住日公告，考查到达时段的分配依据、禁带物品及要求尽快移车的原因。

sets.passage: Listen to an announcement about the university's winter weather policy. With the cold season upon us, I'd like to review how the university handles severe weather closures so you know what to expect. Whenever classes are cancelled or delayed because of snow or ice, the decision will be announced by six in the morning through the emergency text-alert system, the university homepage, and local radio stations. If you haven't signed up for text alerts, please do so today; it's the fastest way to hear the news and takes only a minute on the portal. Remember that a delayed opening does not mean classes are cancelled, only that they start later, following a special schedule posted online. Even when the university remains open, we understand that commuting students may face unsafe roads, and no one will be penalized for staying home when travel is genuinely dangerous; simply notify your instructors. Essential services, including the dining halls and the health center, stay open during closures, staffed by employees who live on or near campus. When in doubt, prioritize your safety over perfect attendance.

questions.prompt: By what time will weather closures be announced?
What does a delayed opening mean?
options: Classes start later on a special schedule

questions.prompt: What does the speaker suggest students prioritize during dangerous weather?

sets.explain_zh: 冬季恶劣天气政策公告，考查停课公布时间、延迟开课的含义及危险天气下应优先考虑的事（安全）。

sets.passage: Listen to an announcement about a new campus bike-share program. Attention, everyone who's tired of walking across our sprawling campus in the rain: a new bike-share program launches next week, and it's completely free for students. We've installed twelve docking stations at key locations, including the dorms, the library, the athletic complex, and the far end of the science quad. To check out a bike, download the campus mobility app, scan the code on the handlebars, and ride to any station you like. There's no charge for trips under an hour, which comfortably covers any journey across our campus; longer trips incur a small fee to keep bikes circulating rather than sitting idle in someone's dorm room. Helmets are strongly recommended and can be borrowed at no cost from the recreation center front desk. Please ride only on the marked bike lanes and pathways, never on the pedestrian-only walkways near the main plaza, where collisions with people on foot are a real concern. If a bike seems damaged, report it in the app rather than riding it, and we'll swap it out promptly.

questions.prompt: How long can a trip last before a fee applies?
Where can students borrow a helmet for free?
options: At the recreation center front desk

questions.prompt: Why does the speaker charge a fee for longer trips?
options: To make a profit for the university
To keep bikes circulating rather than sitting idle
To discourage students from riding at all
To pay for the mobility app

sets.explain_zh: 共享单车项目公告，考查免费骑行时长、免费借头盔地点及长途收费的原因（保持车辆流通）。

sets.passage: Listen to an announcement about an upcoming theater production. Good evening, theater lovers. The university drama department is proud to present its winter production, an original comedy written and directed entirely by our own students. Performances run for two weekends, with evening shows on Fridays and Saturdays at seven-thirty and a matinee each Sunday at two. Tickets are available now at the box office in the arts building lobby and through the department's website. Students attend free with a valid identification card, but here's the catch: free student tickets must be reserved in advance and picked up at least thirty minutes before curtain, or they'll be released to the waiting list. General admission for the public is ten dollars, with proceeds supporting next season's productions. Please arrive early, as latecomers cannot be seated until a suitable break in the performance, out of respect for the actors and your fellow audience members. And a gentle reminder to silence your phones completely; even a screen lighting up in a dark theater is distracting to the performers just a few feet away. We hope you'll join us for a memorable evening.

questions.prompt: What must students do to attend for free?
options: Reserve tickets in advance and pick them up thirty minutes before curtain

questions.prompt: When are the matinee performances?
Why does the speaker ask the audience to silence their phones completely?
options: Because even a lit screen distracts the nearby performers
To prevent recording of the show

sets.explain_zh: 戏剧演出公告，考查学生免费观演的条件、日场时间及要求彻底静音手机的原因。

sets.passage: Listen to an announcement about financial aid deadlines. This is an important reminder from the Office of Financial Aid regarding next year's funding. The priority deadline to submit your renewal application is the first of March, and I cannot stress enough how much this date matters. Students who apply by the priority deadline are considered for the full range of grants, work-study positions, and institutional scholarships. Those who apply even a week later are still eligible for loans, but much of the free money, the aid you never have to repay, will already have been awarded. In other words, missing this deadline could literally cost you thousands of dollars. The application takes most students under an hour, especially if you gather your tax documents beforehand. If your family's financial situation has changed recently, perhaps due to a job loss, you may submit a special-circumstances appeal, and our counselors will review your case individually. Walk-in advising hours are available every afternoon this month, and we've added evening sessions on Tuesdays for students who work during the day. Please don't wait until the last minute.

questions.prompt: What is the priority deadline?
What are late applicants still eligible for?
Why does the speaker say that missing the deadline could cost thousands of dollars?
options: To emphasize the financial importance of applying on time

sets.explain_zh: 助学金截止日期提醒，考查优先截止日期、迟交者仍可获得的资助及强调金额损失的用意。

sets.passage: Listen to an announcement from the campus technology office. Attention, campus community. Over the upcoming three-day weekend, our information-technology team will be performing a major upgrade to the campus wireless network. As a result, internet and network services across all buildings will be intermittently unavailable from Friday evening through Monday morning. We know this timing isn't perfect, but we deliberately chose the holiday weekend precisely because far fewer people are on campus and fewer classes are affected. When you return on Tuesday, you'll notice a single new network name replacing the three confusing ones we've had for years. You'll need to reconnect your devices once using your student login, after which they'll connect automatically from then on. The upgrade should roughly double connection speeds in the residence halls, where slow evening service has been a frequent and understandable complaint. During the outage, the library will keep a small number of wired computer stations running for anyone with an urgent deadline. If you experience problems reconnecting after the upgrade, the technology help desk will be fully staffed all day Tuesday. Thank you for your patience during this improvement.

questions.prompt: When will network services be disrupted?
options: From Friday evening through Monday morning

questions.prompt: What improvement will residence-hall students notice?
Why did the IT team choose the holiday weekend for the upgrade?
options: Because staff are paid more then
Because the help desk is closed
Because fewer people are on campus, affecting fewer classes

sets.explain_zh: 校园无线网络升级公告，考查中断时段、宿舍网速改善及选在假期升级的原因。

sets.passage: Listen to an announcement about the campus day of service. Hello, everyone. Mark your calendars for the university's annual Day of Service, coming up on the last Saturday of the month. Every year, hundreds of students, staff, and faculty spend the morning volunteering at sites throughout the surrounding community. This year's projects include planting trees in the riverside park, sorting donations at the food bank, painting classrooms at a local elementary school, and visiting residents at a senior center. When you register online, you'll choose your preferred project, though popular sites fill up fast, so signing up early gives you the best chance. Breakfast and a free event T-shirt are provided to all volunteers who check in by eight in the morning at the student center. Transportation to each site is arranged, so you won't need a car. We ask that you dress appropriately for your chosen activity; outdoor projects, in particular, call for sturdy shoes and clothes you don't mind getting dirty. More than the free shirt or the breakfast, though, what students consistently tell us afterward is how meaningful it feels to connect with our neighbors.

questions.prompt: What is provided to volunteers who check in by eight?
How do volunteers get to their service sites?
options: They take public buses at their own cost

questions.prompt: According to the speaker, what do students value most about the day?

sets.explain_zh: 校园志愿服务日公告，考查签到福利、前往地点的交通安排及学生最看重的收获（与社区连接）。

sets.passage: Listen to a professor explain the final exam procedures. Let's take a few minutes to go over exactly how our final exam will work next week, so there are no surprises on the day. The exam is scheduled for Thursday at nine in the morning in our usual classroom, not in a special exam hall, so come to the room you already know. Please arrive a few minutes early and leave your bags at the front; the only items you'll need at your desk are pens and a simple calculator. Note that the calculator must be a basic model; anything with internet access or a full keyboard is not permitted, and I'll be checking. The exam covers everything from the second half of the course, since the material from the first half was already tested at midterm. You'll have exactly two hours, and I won't grant extensions except for documented emergencies arranged in advance. If you finish early, you may leave quietly, but once you hand in your exam, you cannot return to it. Get a good night's sleep beforehand; a rested mind will serve you far better than a few extra hours of frantic cramming.

questions.prompt: Where will the final exam take place?
What kind of calculator is permitted?
options: A basic model without internet or a full keyboard

questions.prompt: What does the professor imply students should do the night before?
options: Cram for a few extra hours
Review the first half of the course
Arrive early at a special exam hall

sets.explain_zh: 教授讲解期末考试安排，考查考试地点、允许的计算器类型及暗示考前应做的事（好好睡觉）。

sets.passage: Listen to an announcement about the new student center. We're delighted to announce that the long-awaited Marlowe Student Center opens its doors this Monday, and we'd love for you to explore it. The three-story building brings together services that used to be scattered across campus. On the ground floor you'll find a food court with six vendors, comfortable lounge seating, and a stage for performances and club events. The second floor houses the offices of more than forty student organizations, along with meeting rooms any registered club can reserve for free through the campus app. The top floor is dedicated to quiet study, with individual carrels and a wall of windows overlooking the quad. To celebrate the opening, there will be free refreshments and live music all day Monday, plus a raffle for campus store gift cards. One practical note: the building uses your student identification card for entry after ten at night, so late-night studiers, be sure to carry your card. We think you'll find the center becomes the natural heart of campus life, and we can't wait to see how you use it.

questions.prompt: What is on the top floor of the student center?
options: A food court with six vendors

questions.prompt: How can clubs reserve the second-floor meeting rooms?
options: For free through the campus app
At the front desk in person

questions.prompt: Why does the speaker remind students to carry their ID card at night?
options: Because it is required for entry after ten at night
To buy food at the food court

sets.explain_zh: 新学生中心开放公告，考查顶层功能、社团预订会议室方式及夜间需带学生证的原因。

sets.passage: Listen to a guided tour of the campus botanical garden. Welcome to the university's botanical garden, and thank you for spending your afternoon with us. As we stroll along the main path, please stay on the gravel walkways, since the soil beds are delicate and easily compacted by footsteps. Our garden spans nearly ten acres and is organized into themed sections; we'll pass through the desert collection, the rose terrace, and finally the greenhouse, which stays warm and humid year-round. Keep an eye out for the small signs beside each plant, which give both the common and scientific names, along with the region the plant originally comes from. Many visitors are surprised to learn that a number of these species are endangered in the wild, and part of our mission is preserving them here for future generations. If you'd like to get involved, we welcome student volunteers every weekend, no gardening experience necessary; we'll teach you everything. Photography is welcome throughout the garden. Toward the end, we'll pause at the pond, a lovely spot to rest, and I'll answer any questions you've saved up along the way.

questions.prompt: Why are visitors asked to stay on the gravel walkways?
options: Because the soil beds are delicate and easily compacted

questions.prompt: What information do the signs beside each plant provide?
options: Common and scientific names and the plant's region of origin

questions.prompt: Why does the speaker mention that some species are endangered in the wild?
options: To warn visitors not to touch them
To explain part of the garden's preservation mission

sets.explain_zh: 植物园导览，考查要求走碎石道的原因、植物标牌信息及提及濒危物种的用意（保护使命）。
